atoms.content: Linear Regression predicts a continuous numerical output by fitting a straight 'line of best fit' through the data points.
Imagine drawing a single straight line through a swarm of bees that represents the overall direction the swarm is flying.
The model learns by minimizing a Cost Function (usually Mean Squared Error), which measures how far the predicted line is from the actual data points.
Gradient Descent is the optimization algorithm used to iteratively tweak the line's slope and intercept until the error is as small as possible.

1. Predicts continuous numbers.
2. Uses a line of best fit.
3. Fast and highly interpretable.
Can Linear Regression be used to predict if an email is Spam or Not Spam? (Answer: No, it is for predicting numbers, not categories).
Despite its name, Logistic Regression is a classification algorithm used to predict binary outcomes (Yes/No, True/False) by outputting a probability between 0 and 1.
Instead of a straight line going to infinity, Logistic Regression uses an S-shaped curve that squashes all predictions into a strict percentage chance (0% to 100%).
The math behind the S-curve is called the Sigmoid function. If the output probability is > 0.5, it classifies the result as 'Yes'.

1. Used for binary classification.
2. Outputs probabilities.
3. Uses the Sigmoid curve.
What mathematical function does Logistic Regression use to keep its predictions strictly between 0 and 1? (Answer: The Sigmoid function).
A Decision Tree classifies data by answering a series of sequential Yes/No questions, splitting the dataset into smaller branches until it reaches a final decision.
It works exactly like a game of '20 Questions'. Does it have fur? (Yes) Does it bark? (Yes) -> It's a dog.
The tree decides which question to ask first by using math (Gini Impurity or Entropy) to find the question that most perfectly separates the data categories.
Decision trees are highly prone to overfitting because, if left unconstrained, they will keep asking questions until every single training data point has its own tiny branch.

1. Highly interpretable.
2. No data scaling required.
3. Extremely prone to overfitting if not pruned.
Does a Decision Tree require you to use Min-Max scaling on your features before training? (Answer: No, trees do not rely on distances between points).
A Random Forest is an 'ensemble' model that trains hundreds of separate Decision Trees on random subsets of the data, and then has them vote on the final prediction.
A single Decision Tree is asking one friend for advice. A Random Forest is asking 500 friends for advice and going with the majority vote.
This technique is called Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating). Because the trees are trained on random subsets, they don't make the exact same mistakes, making the forest highly accurate.

1. Ensemble of many trees.
2. Fixes the overfitting problem of single trees.
3. Very accurate but less interpretable.
What do we call a machine learning model (like Random Forest) that combines the predictions of multiple smaller models? (Answer: An Ensemble model).
SVM is a classification algorithm that finds the widest possible 'street' (margin) between different categories of data to confidently separate them.
Imagine trying to put a thick piece of tape on the floor between red balls and blue balls. You want the tape to be as wide as possible without touching any balls.
The specific data points resting exactly on the edges of this 'street' are called support vectors. They alone define the entire boundary.
If data can't be separated by a straight line, SVM uses the 'Kernel Trick' to mathematically project the data into a higher 3D dimension where a straight cut is possible.

1. Maximizes the margin between classes.
2. Uses Kernels for complex data.
3. Excellent for high-dimensional data like text.
In an SVM, do data points that are far away from the boundary line affect where the line is drawn? (Answer: No, only the 'support vectors' on the edge matter).
KNN classifies a new data point by looking at the 'K' closest data points in the training set and having them vote on the new point's category based on distance.
If you move to a new neighborhood, you're likely similar to your closest neighbors. If 4 of your 5 closest neighbors vote Democrat, KNN predicts you do too.
KNN is a 'lazy learner.' It doesn't actually build an internal mathematical model during training; it just memorizes the data and does all the math during prediction.

1. Distance-based (requires feature scaling!).
2. No training phase (Lazy).
3. Slow at prediction time.
Because KNN relies entirely on measuring the distance between points, what critical preprocessing step must you do to your data first? (Answer: Feature Scaling).
Naive Bayes is a lightning-fast probability algorithm based on Bayes' Theorem. It calculates the probability of a class given a set of features.
It acts like a detective making a snap judgment based on clues. "I see the word 'Free' and 'Winner', so I'm 99% sure this email is Spam."

1. Assumes all features are perfectly independent (which is 'naive').
2. Incredibly fast.
3. Industry standard for text classification.
Why is the algorithm called 'Naive'? (Answer: Because it naively assumes all features in the dataset have zero correlation with each other).
It computes the probability of each class given the input features, then picks the class with the highest probability. Prior probability is updated with new evidence.
GaussianNB works with continuous data (assumes bell curve). MultinomialNB works with word counts (text classification). BernoulliNB works with binary features (yes/no).
Gradient Boosting is an ensemble technique that trains Decision Trees sequentially. Each new tree specifically tries to fix the exact errors made by the previous tree.
It's like taking a test. You fail a few questions, so you hire a tutor specifically for the ones you failed. Then you hire another tutor for the remaining failures.
XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) is an optimized, blazingly fast software library implementing this algorithm. It is considered the gold standard for tabular data in ML competitions.
Random Forest builds trees independently and at the same time (Bagging). XGBoost builds trees sequentially, one after the other (Boosting).

1. Sequential learning.
2. Dominates Kaggle tabular competitions.
3. Highly prone to overfitting if not tuned carefully.
Does XGBoost train its Decision Trees simultaneously in parallel, or sequentially one by one? (Answer: Sequentially).
